answer: Magna Carta (Latin for "Great Charter"), also called Magna Carta Libertatum, meaning "the Great Charter of Freedoms", is a charter agreed to by King John of England at Runnymede, near Windsor, on 15 June 1215. First drafted by the Archbishop of Canterbury to make peace between the unpopular King and a group of rebel barons, it promised the protection of church rights, protection for the barons from illegal imprisonment, access to swift justice, and limitations on feudal payments to the Crown, to be implemented through a council of 25 barons. Neither side stood behind their commitments, and the charter was annulled by the Pope later that year. The barons then pressured King John into accepting the Magna Carta (meaning "Great Charter") in 1216, which re-issued the provisions of the original and confirmed individual rights.

USER:
answer: Peripheral artery disease (PAD), also known as peripheral arterial disease, is a condition in which narrowed arteries reduce blood flow to your limbs. When you develop PAD, your extremities — usually your legs — don't get enough blood flow to keep up with demand. This causes symptoms, most notably leg pain when walking (intermittent claudication). Without enough blood flow, your risk of heart attack or stroke increases. PAD is a form of atherosclerosis, a condition that occurs when a waxy substance called plaque builds up on the walls of your arteries. The buildup narrows your arteries, reducing blood flow to your limbs.

answer: The Handmaid's Tale is a 1985 dystopian novel by Canadian author Margaret Atwood. answer: Anti-nuclear antibody (ANA) is an antibody that is produced against nuclear antigens. These antigens are found in the nucleus of almost all nucleated cells and are usually not expressed on the surface of cells. The presence of antibodies against these antigens is referred to as the presence of antinuclear antibodies (ANA). ANA are found in many autoimmune diseases. They are found in 95% of systemic lupus erythematosus, 90% of scleroderma, 80% of mixed connective tissue disease, and 50% of polymyositis. They are also found in 30% of rheumatoid arthritis and 10% of healthy people.

answer: The Blacklist (TV series) is an American crime drama television series that premiered on NBC on September 23, 2013. answer: G20 or Group of Twenty is an international forum for the governments and central bank governors from 20 major economies. answer: Justinian I (Latin: Iustinianus; Greek: Ἰουστινιανός, Ioustinianós; 482 – 14 November 565), also known as Justinian the Great and Justinian I the Great, was Eastern Roman Emperor from 527 to 565. answer: Perth is the capital and largest city of the Australian state of Western Australia (WA). It is the fourth most populous city in Australia, with a population of 2.06 million living in Greater Perth. Perth is part of the South West Land Division of Western Australia, with the majority of the metropolitan area located on the Swan Coastal Plain, a narrow strip between the Indian Ocean and the Darling Scarp, a low range that extends from east to west across the state. The first European to sight Perth was Dutch explorer Willem de Vlamingh, in 1697, on 12 March, the anniversary of which is celebrated as the city's birthday. The first European settlement of the area went ahead in 1829, when the British founded the Swan River Colony, and achieved separation from the Colony of New South Wales in 1850. The city has expanded outwards and upwards along the Swan Coastal Plain, with the population increasing tenfold in the second half of the 20th century, and doubled in the 30 years between 1981 and 2011. Its local government area is the City of Perth, which is governed by the Perth City Council; it is one of three City of Perth municipalities that together comprise the greater metropolitan area of Perth.

answer: Cerebral palsy (CP) is a group of permanent movement disorders that appear in early childhood. Signs and symptoms vary between people. Often, symptoms include poor coordination, stiff muscles, weak muscles, and tremors. These symptoms usually involve the legs, arms, and trunk, but can also affect other parts of the body. There may also be vision problems and seizures. Cerebral palsy is caused by abnormal brain development or damage to the developing brain. This can occur before, during, or after birth. Risk factors include complications during pregnancy, premature birth, low birth weight, and infections in the newborn. The underlying cause is often unknown. The diagnosis is based on a person's signs and symptoms. In children, the diagnosis is often delayed. Genetic testing may be done to look for genes that are known to cause the condition. There is no cure for cerebral palsy, but treatment can help improve function. Treatment may include physical therapy, occupational therapy, speech therapy, and medications. Surgery may be recommended in some cases.
